title: Air-fryer steak
subtitle: Ribeye or sirloin, medium-rare in 10 minutes
excerpt: A ribeye or sirloin steak cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for 10 minutes total for medium-rare. The air fryer produces a browned, slightly textured crust without a pan and without smoke. The steak must rest for the same time it cooked: 5 minutes per side.
sourceNotes: Steak cooked in an air fryer became popular as a smoke-free alternative to pan-frying at high heat. The air fryer does not replicate the Maillard crust of a very hot cast-iron pan, but it produces an evenly browned surface with minimal equipment and no ventilation issues. The ribeye is the best choice because the fat cap self-bastes the meat during cooking.

content.text: Bring the steak to room temperature before cooking. A steak straight from the fridge has a cold centre that takes longer to reach the target temperature, meaning the outside overcooks while the inside catches up. Twenty minutes at room temperature fixes this entirely.

items.prepNote: at least 2.5 cm thick, at room temperature

content.text: Take the {{beef-ribeye}} out of the fridge 20 minutes before cooking. Pat dry, rub with {{vegetable-oil}}, and season generously with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}} on all sides including the fat cap. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C for 5 minutes. Place the steaks in the basket, fat-cap side down first for 1 minute, then lay flat. Cook at 200°C for 5 minutes on the first side, flip, and cook for a further 4 to 5 minutes for medium-rare (internal temperature 54–57°C). Remove and rest on a warm plate for 5 minutes, placing the {{unsalted-butter}} on top if using. The carry-over heat will bring the internal temperature up by 3 to 4°C during the rest.

items.symptom: Steak grey throughout with no visible crust
items.cause: Air fryer not preheated, or steak cooked straight from the fridge
items.fix: Always preheat the air fryer for at least 5 minutes before the steak goes in. A hot basket produces a more definite crust. Room temperature steak is essential for even cooking

items.symptom: Steak very dry and chewy after resting
items.fix: Use a thermometer. Medium-rare is 54 to 57°C; medium is 60 to 63°C. Ribeye has enough fat to stay palatable at medium but becomes noticeably tougher at well done

items.symptom: Steak smells burnt but looks fine
items.cause: Fat dripping onto the heating element
items.fix: A small amount of smoke from fat drips is normal in the air fryer. Ensure the drip tray is clean before cooking. Ribeye produces more smoke than leaner cuts; a well-ventilated kitchen helps

content.text: Air-fryer steak is the weeknight alternative to a cast-iron pan and extractor fan. The result is not identical to pan-fried: the Maillard crust is less intense, but it produces far less smoke. Serve with air-fryer chips, roast tomatoes, and a watercress salad.
